fusion_decision_raw.rationale: Sado's Father, Mother, Wife and Son all serve the single dramatic function of household-hospitality and warning; per CF-01 (each <2 scenes, identical kunak function) their gestures and the father's warning proverb collapse into Sado himself, giving the saklya one moral voice instead of four — Old Man of Saklya likewise absorbed as prologue-variant of the same hosting function.

Absorb Manana Orbelyani's single intervention at the Tiflis dinner into Marya Vasilevna; she already fulfills the identical dramatic function (social moderator at the imperial salon) and a feature script cannot carry two interchangeable hostess figures.

Chunk chunk_027: Princess, Vorontsov's wife; elegantly dressed, socially adept, charmed by Hadji Murad's flush when she extended her hand

fusion_decision_raw.rationale: Abu Nutsal Khan and Umma Khan share an identical dramatic function (Khansha's son, foster-bonded to HM, doomed victim of Hamzad's massacre, fewer than 5 scenes each); per CF-01 and CF-09 the two collapse into a single compound 'eldest Khan son' carried by Umma Khan, whose sworn-brother bond with HM is the more dramatically potent vessel and whose name retains historical specificity, while Abu Nutsal's pride-and-shame beat (the mother's taunt, the refusal to heed HM's warning) is transferred onto Umma Khan as the trigger that walks him into the trap.

Chunk chunk_050: Eldest son of the Khansha; proud and easily shamed into dangerous action — the Khansha calls him afraid and he rides to Hamzad despite Hadji Murad's warning; headed toward the massacre.

fusion_decision_raw.rationale: Umma Khan and Abu Nutsal Khan share an identical dramatic function in the Hamzad-massacre flashback (lured Khan-brother hostage, doomed patron of HM's youth); collapsing them into a single composite Khan-brother preserves the trap's escalation and HM's guilt while honouring CF-01 (<5 scenes, shared function) and CF-09 (scene economy).

arc_notes_first2: Chunk chunk_048: One of three young Khans; Hadji Murad's sworn brother from the Khansha's palace; part of childhood memory.
Chunk chunk_049: Second son of the old Khansha; sent with Hadji Murad to Tiflis to seek Russian help; physically strong and brave but weak-willed, corrupted by Russian officers

fusion_decision_raw.rationale: 'Baron Rosen' and 'General Rosen' (idx 12) are the same historical person — Baron Grigory Vladimirovich Rosen, Russian Commander-in-Chief in the Caucasus before Vorontsov; one mention each, identical dramatic function (imperial gatekeeper who refuses Hadji Murad), no thematic contrast lost by collapsing the two surface-name variants into a single canonical entry.

fuse_recommendations.justification: I was sent here to hold a frontier, not to arbitrate blood feuds between mountain chieftains; verbal assurances are all the Empire can afford to give men who will be dead by next winter.

fusion_decision_raw.rationale: Aseldar appears in only one chunk as a single-action backstory figure (kills the Khansha, dies in Hamzad's mosque ambush); his dramatic function (Hamzad-loyalist murid executing the dynastic massacre) is identical to Kazi-Mulla's, and feature compression cannot sustain a named role with one mention — collapse the action into Kazi-Mulla as the composite Hamzad-murid, or cut entirely if the mosque massacre is rendered as exposition rather than scene.

fuse_recommendations.justification: The khans are corrupt collaborators with the infidel and the Khansha protects their line; killing her at Hamzad's word is not murder but the will of God enacted through a faithful hand.

fusion_decision_raw.rationale: Historically identical figure (Baron Grigory Vladimirovich Rosen, Russian commander in the Caucasus); both entries refer to the same general who granted Hadji Murad his commission and authority over Avaria — keeping them separate would duplicate one historical anchor.

fuse_recommendations.justification: I gave this mountain wolf the rank of an officer and Avaria itself — order in the Caucasus is bought one chieftain at a time, and any general who refuses that arithmetic loses the line.

fusion_decision.rationale: Protagonist and historical anchor; cannot be fused with any other figure under novel_to_feature compression.

fusion_decision.rationale: Eldar is THE archetypal murid — HM's closest body-double in loyalty and the one who dies beside him in the final ambush; he is the figure into whom other minor murids (Khanefi's song, Gamzalo's preparation beats) are absorbed, not the figure to be dissolved, and his silent visual presence carries the film's central image of unbought devotion across all 29 of his chunks.

arc_notes_first2: Chunk chunk_005: Hadji Murad's murid (devoted follower); young, silent, with fine ram-like eyes; sits cross-legged and observes the old man's talk.

fusion_decision.rationale: Khan Mahoma carries a distinct frivolous-impulsive voice and the dramatic function of HM's outward-facing envoy/messenger murid; collapsing him into Khanefi (earnest) or Bata (outsider guide) would erase the contrast inside HM's murid quartet and remove the only murid whose levity humanizes the group before the final battle.

fusion_decision.rationale: Panov is the dramatically active comrade in the Avdeev death-arc and the informational anchor near Butler at the assault — he carries dialogue, decisions, and the letter-writing scene; the proposed merge partners (Serogin, Nikitin, Bondarenko, Old Sergeant-Major) are functionally absorbable INTO Panov, not the other way around, so Panov stays as the canonical NCO-witness and the others fold upward.

arc_notes_first2: Chunk chunk_009: Non-commissioned officer on night ambush duty; pragmatic, leads soldiers in casual camp behavior; addresses Avdeev familiarly

fusion_decision.rationale: The Avdeev episode is a self-contained symbolic counterweight to the imperial dispatch — fusing him into another soldier would erase Tolstoy's anti-imperial statement about the namelessness of peasant casualties.

arc_notes_first2: Chunk chunk_010: Russian soldier, cheerful but plagued by existential dullness; admits to drinking away his sadness; confides openly about despair.

fusion_decision.rationale: Simon Vorontsov and his father Mikhail Vorontsov occupy distinct dramatic functions — Simon is the personal host who pledges his word in his own tent, Mikhail is the distant Viceroy whose institutional authority overrides that word; collapsing them would erase the father-son betrayal vector that gives the Vorontsov plotline its Tolstoian moral weight, and the suggested fusion with Marya Vasilevna is a CUT-not-fusion (her thematic Baryatinsky subplot belongs elsewhere or is dropped, not absorbed into a male officer).

fusion_decision.rationale: Poltoratsky is the load-bearing Russian-officer POV that bridges the Tiflis salon world (Marya Vasilevna, card play, infatuation) with the active Caucasus front (woodcutting expedition, fire beyond the Shagirinsk Gate); collapsing him into Butler, Avdeev or Major Petrov would either fuse incompatible functions (Butler is the late-novel disillusioned newcomer; Avdeev is the doomed peasant-soldier; the Major is the cynical Mentor-Herald) or destroy the salon-to-front bridge that no other single character carries — instead, the smaller satellites Adjutant, Tikhonov and Freze can be absorbed into Poltoratsky per the extraction notes.

fusion_decision.rationale: Among the five murids around Hadji Murad, Gamzalo holds a distinct dramatic function — the grim, scarred veteran whose silent readiness embodies the cost of loyalty — which neither Khanefi (logistics) nor Eldar (younger follower) can carry without flattening the inner-circle's tonal range.

His chief reason for exchanging was that he had lost all he had at cards and was...

Chunk chunk_107: ...hich the regimental singers performed and which ended up with a drinking bout.  Major Petrov, very drunk and no longer red but ash pale, sat astride a chair and, drawing his sword, hacked at imaginary foes, alternately swearing and laughi...
